abstract: This paper investigates how pro-government Telegram channels in Russia and Belarus engage in coordinated information-sharing behavior to amplify regime-aligned narratives. Using network analysis of message-forwarding patterns, the study identifies clusters of channels operating in apparent coordination. The findings illuminate how authoritarian information ecosystems leverage Telegram's affordances to propagate state-aligned content.
key_claims: Pro-government Telegram channels in Russia and Belarus exhibit identifiable patterns of coordinated activity rather than acting independently.
Coordination among these channels serves to amplify regime-aligned narratives within authoritarian information environments.
Telegram's platform affordances, particularly message forwarding, enable and structure this coordinated amplification.
methods: Analysis of pro-government Telegram channels operating in Russia and Belarus.
Identification of coordinated behavior through patterns of content sharing and forwarding, likely using network-analytic techniques.
findings: Distinct clusters of pro-government channels engage in coordinated information sharing.
Coordination patterns reveal regime-aligned amplification strategies on Telegram in both Russia and Belarus.
contributions: Provides empirical evidence of coordinated pro-government activity on Telegram, a platform less studied than Western mainstream social media.
Extends research on coordinated inauthentic or coordinated information behavior into authoritarian contexts.
Adds comparative insight into how Russian and Belarusian regime-aligned actors operate online.
framing: The paper situates itself within research on coordinated online behavior, computational propaganda, and authoritarian information control, with particular attention to Telegram as a key political communication platform in post-Soviet authoritarian regimes.
